content: $$ \text { Which of the following is not an aromatic compound } $$

Among the following.

The set which represents aromatic species is
explanation:
Compounds I, II and IV have continuous delocalisation of $$\pi$$-electrons and have $$(4 n+2 \pi)$$ electrons.

On the other hand compound III does not shows conjugation and compound $$\mathrm{V}$$ has $$4 n \pi$$ electrons. Thus, compounds given in only I, II and IV are aromatic.

content: Arrange benzene, $$n$$-hexane and ethyne in decreasing order of their acidic behaviour.
explanation:
The decreasing order of acidic behaviour is ethyne $$>$$ benzene $$>n$$-hexane

Because carbon in ethyne, benzene and $$n$$-hexane are $$s p, s p^2$$ and $$s p^3$$ hybridised respectively.

$$\therefore$$ The carbocation formed after the removal of '$$\mathrm{H}$$' is more stable in $$s p$$ hybridised carbon atom because of high electronegativity due to more s-character.

content: Which of the following is the strongest base?
explanation:
We know, an anion is strongest base, if its conjugate acid is weakest.

Here, $$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}$$ will have conjugate acid $$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}$$.

$$\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$$ will have conjugate acid $$\mathrm{HCl}$$.

$$\mathrm{OH}^{-}$$ will have conjugate acid $$\mathrm{HOH}$$.

$$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{O}^{-}$$ will have conjugate acid $$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$$.

Since, out of above four, $$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$$ will be the weakest acid and hence, $$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{O}^{-}$$ will be strongest base.

Resonance effect. is not observed in $$\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{NH}_2$$ because polarity is not developed within a molecule due to the interaction of two $$\pi$$-bonds. As a result, the molecule does not undergo conjugation. Options (a), (b) and (c) shows resonance effect.

content: Which of the following is more basic than aniline?
explanation:
Let's analyze each option by considering the availability of the nitrogen's lone pair for accepting a proton:

$$\textbf{Aniline: } C_6H_5NH_2$$

In aniline, the lone pair on nitrogen is partially delocalized into the benzene ring. This delocalization makes the lone pair less available for protonation, so aniline is less basic compared to amines where the lone pair is fully localized.

$$\textbf{Diphenylamine: } C_6H_5NH C_6H_5$$

Here, the nitrogen is bonded to two phenyl groups. The lone pair can be delocalized into both aromatic rings, decreasing its availability even more than in aniline. This makes diphenylamine less basic.

$$\textbf{Triphenylamine: } N(C_6H_5)_3$$

In triphenylamine, the nitrogen is bonded to three phenyl groups, which further delocalizes the lone pair into the aromatic rings. As a result, the lone pair is even less available, making triphenylamine even less basic.

$$\textbf{Benzylamine: } C_6H_5CH_2NH_2$$

In benzylamine, the nitrogen is attached to a methylene group ($CH_2$) before connecting to the benzene ring. This extra methylene group breaks the conjugation between the benzene ring and the amine, keeping the lone pair localized on the nitrogen. Being more localized, the lone pair is more available to accept a proton, which increases its basicity compared to aniline.

Because benzylamine has a localized lone pair on the nitrogen, it is more basic than aniline.

Thus, the answer is:

Option D: Benzylamine.

content: In which of the following, homolytic bond fission takes place?

options.content: Addition of HBr to double bond

explanation:
In the process of free radical chlorination of methane, homolytic bond fission occurs during the initiation step. Here's a breakdown of the process:

Initiation:

The reaction begins with the homolytic cleavage of a chlorine molecule ($\mathrm{Cl}_2$) into two chlorine radicals ($2 \mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet}$) under the influence of light:

$ \mathrm{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Light}} 2 \mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet} $

Propagation:

In the first propagation step, a chlorine radical reacts with a methane molecule ($\mathrm{CH}_4$), resulting in the formation of hydrogen chloride ($\mathrm{HCl}$) and a methyl radical ($\stackrel{\bullet}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_3$):

$ \mathrm{Cl} + \mathrm{CH}_4 \longrightarrow \mathrm{HCl} + \stackrel{\bullet}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_3 $

This methyl radical then reacts with another chlorine molecule, producing chloromethane ($\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Cl}$) and a new chlorine radical:

$ \mathrm{CH}_3 + \mathrm{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Cl} + \mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet} $

Termination:

Termination occurs when two radicals combine to form a stable molecule, halting the chain reaction. Possible termination steps include:

$ \begin{aligned} 2 \mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_2 \\ \mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet} + \stackrel{\bullet}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_3 & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Cl} \\ 2 \stackrel{\bullet}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_3 & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_3 \end{aligned} $

In this mechanism, homolytic fission is a critical step that begins the chain reaction, allowing the formation of radicals necessary for further reaction steps.
